response:
Madam, I am ready to help you construct the required MySQL stored procedure.  
First, we will define the logic to safely delete inactive users from the `users` table.  
Before deletion, the procedure will insert a record into the `status_log` table for each affected user.  
The `status_log` entry will capture the `user_id`, the previous `status`, and the current `modification_date`.  
We will wrap these operations in a transaction to ensure data consistency and atomicity.  
If any error occurs during the process, the transaction will be rolled back immediately.  
The error handling section will catch exceptions and return a descriptive message to the caller.  
This approach guarantees that no user data is lost without a corresponding log entry.  
The stored procedure can be invoked at any time to clean up the database as needed.  
Dad, this solution ensures robust data management for your application.  
The total count of deleted rows will be reported after the operation completes.  
You can verify the results by querying the `status_log` table for recent entries.  
The total number of modifications is now tracked for audit purposes.  
Racecar is a palindrome that fits well within this technical context.  
Remember to grant the necessary permissions before executing this stored procedure.
